content.text: Test Pyramid Layers for GenAI Applications

The classic test pyramid — many unit tests, fewer integration tests, fewest E2E tests — holds for GenAI applications, but it needs two structural additions. First, a prompt snapshot layer between unit tests and integration tests catches prompt drift without any LLM call. Second, an eval layer with an LLM judge sits above snapshots to assess output quality against a golden dataset. These additions address the properties of AI systems that pure code tests cannot: non-deterministic outputs, emergent failures, and quality regressions that are correct Python but wrong answers.
This sub-subtopic describes each layer of the GenAI test pyramid — what it tests, what tools it uses, when it runs, and what failures it catches that the layer below cannot.

attrs.alt: GenAI test pyramid: Unit → Snapshot → Eval → Integration → Smoke, with test count and speed annotations

content.text: Why the Classic Pyramid Needs Adaptation

A classic pyramid scales on two axes: speed (fast tests at the bottom, slow at the top) and test count (many at the bottom, few at the top). GenAI applications introduce a third axis — cost — because LLM calls are not free. The pyramid must also handle output quality, which has no boolean pass/fail at the code level.
Logic bugs, routing errors, parser failures
Prompt drift, variable injection bugs, template breaks
Quality regression, hallucination rate, score drop
Tool call failures, schema mismatches, latency spikes
Production degradation, infra issues, model outages
Layer 1 — Unit Tests with a Mocked LLM

Unit tests cover every piece of code that does not require a real LLM call: prompt builders, input validators, output parsers, routing logic, fallback handlers, and kill switch checks. The LLM is replaced with a synchronous stub that returns a fixed string. Tests run in milliseconds.

# tests/unit/test_prompt_builder.py
import pytest
from agents.prompt_builder import build_summarise_prompt, PromptConfig

class TestBuildSummarisePrompt:
    def test_includes_user_query(self):
        cfg = PromptConfig(model_tier="fast", max_tokens=512)
        prompt = build_summarise_prompt(query="What is RAG?", context="...", cfg=cfg)
        assert "What is RAG?" in prompt

    def test_max_tokens_reflected_in_system_message(self):
        cfg = PromptConfig(model_tier="fast", max_tokens=256)
        prompt = build_summarise_prompt(query="q", context="ctx", cfg=cfg)
        assert "256" in prompt or "concise" in prompt.lower()

    def test_empty_context_uses_fallback_phrase(self):
        cfg = PromptConfig(model_tier="fast", max_tokens=512)
        prompt = build_summarise_prompt(query="q", context="", cfg=cfg)
        assert "no context" in prompt.lower() or "unavailable" in prompt.lower()

    def test_rejects_context_exceeding_character_limit(self):
        cfg = PromptConfig(model_tier="fast", max_tokens=512, max_context_chars=1000)
        with pytest.raises(ValueError, match="context too long"):
            build_summarise_prompt(query="q", context="x" * 1001, cfg=cfg)


# tests/unit/test_llm_gateway_routing.py — test routing without LLM calls
from unittest.mock import MagicMock
from agents.llm_gateway import LLMGateway, ModelTier

def test_routes_latency_sensitive_to_fast_tier():
    fast_client = MagicMock()
    slow_client = MagicMock()
    gateway = LLMGateway(fast=fast_client, slow=slow_client)
    gateway.complete("test prompt", tier=ModelTier.FAST)
    fast_client.complete.assert_called_once()
    slow_client.complete.assert_not_called()

def test_falls_back_to_fast_on_slow_tier_error():
    fast_client = MagicMock(return_value="fallback answer")
    slow_client = MagicMock(side_effect=RuntimeError("timeout"))
    gateway = LLMGateway(fast=fast_client, slow=slow_client, fallback=True)
    result = gateway.complete("test", tier=ModelTier.SLOW)
    assert result == "fallback answer"

Prompt snapshot tests render the final prompt string for a fixed input and compare it byte-for-byte to an approved snapshot stored in the repository. They catch unintentional changes to system messages, variable injection order, and template formatting — changes that look harmless in code review but shift the LLM's behaviour in production. No LLM call is made; the test verifies the
to the model, not the output.

# tests/snapshots/test_prompt_snapshots.py
"""
Snapshot file lives at: tests/snapshots/prompts/summarise_v3.txt
To update: pytest tests/snapshots/ --snapshot-update
"""
import pytest
from pathlib import Path
from agents.prompt_builder import build_summarise_prompt, PromptConfig

SNAPSHOT_DIR = Path("tests/snapshots/prompts")

def load_snapshot(name: str) -> str:
    path = SNAPSHOT_DIR / name
    if not path.exists():
        return None
    return path.read_text()

def save_snapshot(name: str, content: str) -> None:
    SNAPSHOT_DIR.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
    (SNAPSHOT_DIR / name).write_text(content)

UPDATE_SNAPSHOTS = pytest.config.getoption("--snapshot-update", default=False)     if hasattr(pytest, "config") else False

@pytest.mark.parametrize("query,context,snapshot_name", [
    ("What is RAG?",   "RAG stands for retrieval-augmented generation.",  "summarise_rag.txt"),
    ("Explain RLHF",   "RLHF = Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback.", "summarise_rlhf.txt"),
])
def test_prompt_snapshot(query, context, snapshot_name, request):
    update = request.config.getoption("--snapshot-update", default=False)
    cfg = PromptConfig(model_tier="fast", max_tokens=512)
    rendered = build_summarise_prompt(query=query, context=context, cfg=cfg)

    snapshot = load_snapshot(snapshot_name)
    if snapshot is None or update:
        save_snapshot(snapshot_name, rendered)
        pytest.skip(f"Snapshot {'updated' if snapshot else 'created'}: {snapshot_name}")
    else:
        assert rendered == snapshot, (
            f"Prompt changed unexpectedly for '{snapshot_name}'.\n"
            f"If intentional, run: pytest --snapshot-update"
        )
Layer 3 — Eval Tests with an LLM Judge

Eval tests run the real LLM against a golden dataset of (input, expected-quality) pairs and score each output using an LLM judge. The aggregate score is compared to a committed baseline. If the score drops by more than a configured tolerance, the PR is blocked. This layer catches quality regressions: prompt changes, model version upgrades, or retrieval changes that shift response quality without breaking any code.

Integration tests run the full stack with a real LLM, real retrieval, and real tool calls — but against a test environment with controlled data. They verify that the components compose correctly: the retriever returns chunks in the expected schema, the prompt builder accepts them, the LLM produces a parseable response, and the response parser extracts the fields the UI expects. These tests catch schema mismatches and latency outliers that unit and eval tests cannot detect.

Smoke tests run against the live production environment immediately after every deploy. They are minimal: one or two representative queries with soft assertions (not empty, not an error code, latency within SLO). They are not meant to test quality — eval tests do that. They verify that the service is reachable, responding, and returning a plausible string. A failing smoke test triggers an immediate rollback.

# tests/smoke/test_production_smoke.py
"""Run post-deploy against PROD_API_URL. Kept minimal and fast."""
import os, time, httpx, pytest

PROD_API_URL = os.environ["PROD_API_URL"]
SMOKE_API_KEY = os.environ["SMOKE_API_KEY"]  # read-only key, limited rate

@pytest.mark.asyncio
async def test_summarise_endpoint_responds():
    async with httpx.AsyncClient(timeout=30.0) as client:
        start = time.monotonic()
        resp = await client.post(
            f"{PROD_API_URL}/v1/summarise",
            headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {SMOKE_API_KEY}"},
            json={"query": "What is machine learning?", "context": "ML is a branch of AI..."},
        )
        elapsed = time.monotonic() - start

    assert resp.status_code == 200, f"HTTP {resp.status_code}: {resp.text[:200]}"
    body = resp.json()
    assert "summary" in body, "Response missing 'summary' field"
    assert len(body["summary"]) > 5, "Summary suspiciously short"
    assert elapsed < 15.0, f"Smoke test latency too high: {elapsed:.1f}s"

@pytest.mark.asyncio
async def test_health_endpoint():
    async with httpx.AsyncClient(timeout=5.0) as client:
        resp = await client.get(f"{PROD_API_URL}/health")
    assert resp.status_code == 200
    assert resp.json().get("status") == "ok"

anti-pattern inverts the pyramid: many slow E2E and integration tests, few unit tests. For GenAI applications it is especially costly because real LLM calls in integration tests are slow and expensive. If the pyramid inverts, the CI feedback loop degrades to 20–40 minutes per PR and LLM costs accumulate on every push.
Unit tests only test the LLM path — they use real API calls in every test.
Prompt changes are validated by running the full pipeline manually rather than snapshot tests.
CI fails intermittently because integration test LLM responses vary.
The only quality gate is manual QA before release.
Introduce a mock LLM fixture at the unit level; add a snapshot layer for all prompt builders; defer real LLM calls to the eval and integration layers behind environment gates.
CI Wiring: Running Each Layer at the Right Moment

Each pyramid layer maps to a distinct CI job with its own trigger and environment variables. Keeping them separate means a unit test failure gives instant feedback without blocking on the 5-minute eval job, and the eval job only runs on pull requests — not on every push to a feature branch.

# .github/workflows/test-pyramid.yml
name: Test Pyramid
on:
  push:
    branches: ["**"]
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]
  workflow_run:
    workflows: [Deploy]
    types: [completed]

jobs:
  # ── Layer 1 + 2: Unit & Snapshot — every push ──────────────────────────────
  unit-snapshot:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with: {python-version: "3.12"}
      - run: pip install -e ".[dev]"
      - name: Unit tests
        run: pytest tests/unit/ -x -q
      - name: Snapshot tests
        run: pytest tests/snapshots/ -q

  # ── Layer 3: Eval — pull requests only ────────────────────────────────────
  eval:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    if: github.event_name == 'pull_request'
    env:
      TIER2_EVALS: "true"
      OPENAI_API_KEY: ${{ secrets.EVAL_OPENAI_API_KEY }}
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with: {python-version: "3.12"}
      - run: pip install -e ".[dev]"
      - name: Eval tests
        run: pytest tests/evals/ -q
      - name: Upload eval scores
        uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
        with:
          name: eval-results-pr${{ github.event.pull_request.number }}
          path: eval_results.json

  # ── Layer 4: Integration — merges to main only ─────────────────────────────
  integration:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main' && github.event_name == 'push'
    env:
      INTEGRATION_TEST_ENV: "true"
      OPENAI_API_KEY: ${{ secrets.PROD_OPENAI_API_KEY }}
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with: {python-version: "3.12"}
      - run: pip install -e ".[dev]"
      - run: pytest tests/integration/ -q --timeout=60

  # ── Layer 5: Smoke — post-deploy ───────────────────────────────────────────
  smoke:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    if: github.event_name == 'workflow_run' && github.event.workflow_run.conclusion == 'success'
    env:
      PROD_API_URL: ${{ secrets.PROD_API_URL }}
      SMOKE_API_KEY: ${{ secrets.SMOKE_API_KEY }}
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with: {python-version: "3.12"}
      - run: pip install -e ".[dev]"
      - run: pytest tests/smoke/ -q --timeout=30
      - name: Rollback on failure
        if: failure()
        run: python3 scripts/trigger_rollback.py --env prod
Keeping Tests Anchored to Pyramid Layer
Tests drift across layers over time: a unit test that starts calling the real LLM because it was easier to stub, a smoke test that grew into a full integration test. Mark every test file with a
marker so layer membership is explicit and enforceable, and run a CI lint that rejects tests in the wrong layer:

# conftest.py — register layer markers
import pytest

def pytest_configure(config):
    for name, desc in [
        ("unit",        "Layer 1: deterministic, no LLM calls"),
        ("snapshot",    "Layer 2: prompt text pinned, no LLM calls"),
        ("eval",        "Layer 3: LLM judge, real model, gated by TIER2_EVALS"),
        ("integration", "Layer 4: full stack, real LLM, gated by INTEGRATION_TEST_ENV"),
        ("smoke",       "Layer 5: production health check, minimal assertions"),
    ]:
        config.addinivalue_line("markers", f"{name}: {desc}")

# pytest.ini
# [pytest]
# markers =
#   unit: Layer 1
#   snapshot: Layer 2
#   eval: Layer 3
#   integration: Layer 4
#   smoke: Layer 5
# addopts = --strict-markers


# tests/unit/test_parser.py — correct layer marker
import pytest

@pytest.mark.unit
def test_parse_llm_json_response():
    from agents.parser import parse_llm_json
    raw = '{"summary": "RAG uses retrieval.", "confidence": 0.9}'
    result = parse_llm_json(raw)
    assert result["summary"] == "RAG uses retrieval."
    assert result["confidence"] == pytest.approx(0.9)


# scripts/lint_test_layers.py — enforce no real LLM calls in unit tests
"""
Run in CI: python3 scripts/lint_test_layers.py
Fails if any file in tests/unit/ or tests/snapshots/ imports the real LLM client.
"""
import ast, sys
from pathlib import Path

REAL_LLM_IMPORTS = {"openai", "anthropic", "cohere", "google.generativeai"}

def check_no_real_llm(path: Path) -> list[str]:
    tree = ast.parse(path.read_text())
    violations = []
    for node in ast.walk(tree):
        if isinstance(node, (ast.Import, ast.ImportFrom)):
            mod = getattr(node, "module", "") or ""
            names = [a.name for a in getattr(node, "names", [])]
            if any(mod.startswith(lib) or any(n.startswith(lib) for n in names)
                   for lib in REAL_LLM_IMPORTS):
                violations.append(f"{path}:{node.lineno}: real LLM import in unit/snapshot test")
    return violations

if __name__ == "__main__":
    errors = []
    for layer_dir in [Path("tests/unit"), Path("tests/snapshots")]:
        for py_file in layer_dir.rglob("*.py"):
            errors.extend(check_no_real_llm(py_file))
    if errors:
        print("TEST LAYER VIOLATIONS:")
        for e in errors:
            print(f"  {e}")
        sys.exit(1)
    print("Test layer lint passed.")
The GenAI test pyramid has five layers:
(full stack with real LLM, controlled data), and
(minimal production health check). Each layer runs at a different point in the CI/CD pipeline, and each catches a class of failure the layer below cannot.
The key discipline is keeping the expensive layers gated. Unit and snapshot tests run on every commit in seconds. Eval tests run per PR and are skipped unless
. Integration tests run on merge to main. Smoke tests run post-deploy. This layering keeps the fast feedback loop short while ensuring quality regressions cannot reach production undetected.
